name: PostgreSQL - Large Scale Datasets
scope: A person with INTERMEDIATE proficiency level in PostgreSQL - Large Scale Datasets as a competency should be able to explain PostgreSQL architecture (process model, shared buffers, WAL, autovacuum) and its behavior under large data and high-concurrency workloads; interpret table, index, and database size metrics using `pg_class` and `pg_stat_*` views; design scalable schemas, choose data types, manage keys, and plan for schema evolution and audit/history tables; select and maintain appropriate indexes (B-tree, GIN, BRIN), use partial and expression indexes, and analyze index usage; interpret `EXPLAIN`/`EXPLAIN ANALYZE` outputs, optimize queries for planner efficiency, and address anti-patterns in large datasets; implement and manage table partitioning, design partitioning schemes, and handle partition lifecycle and bloat; apply MVCC, select transaction isolation levels, diagnose lock contention, and structure batched/bulk operations to minimize locking; understand and tune autovacuum, diagnose bloat, and schedule maintenance (VACUUM, REINDEX) for large tables; efficiently ingest and transform data using `COPY`, staging tables, and ETL patterns, while managing WAL and index/constraint impacts; configure and monitor streaming replication, understand failover basics, and participate in backup/restore strategies (logical and physical, PITR) for large databases; use PostgreSQL statistics views and monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana), track key metrics (cache hit, replication lag, bloat), and contribute to capacity planning; apply role-based access control, basic encryption, row-level security, and compliance-driven retention/auditing for large datasets; use core PostgreSQL tools (`psql`, `pg_dump`, `pg_basebackup`, `pg_repack`), connection poolers (PgBouncer), migration/versioning tools (Flyway, Liquibase), and integrate with orchestration/monitoring stacks; assist in environment setup, basic configuration tuning, and collaborate on operational practices, documentation, and incident response for large-scale PostgreSQL environments.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core PostgreSQL & Large-Scale Data Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understand how PostgreSQL architecture (process model, shared buffers, WAL, background workers) behaves under large data volume and high-concurrency workloads
- Explain the implications of dataset size on query latency, memory usage, I/O patterns, and maintenance windows
- Distinguish clearly between table size, index size, toast data, and overall database size, and interpret `pg_class` and `pg_stat_*` size metrics
- Understand the role of WAL in durability and recovery, and its impact on I/O throughput for large write workloads
- Describe how checkpoints, full-page writes, and WAL archiving affect performance and storage on large databases
- Understand how autovacuum works conceptually and why it is critical for long-lived, large tables
- Recognize common scaling limits for a single PostgreSQL instance: CPU saturation, IOPS/throughput limits, memory constraints, lock contention, and bloat
- Differentiate vertical scaling (bigger machine) vs horizontal scaling (read replicas, sharding, application-level partitioning) in the PostgreSQL context
- Have a working mental model of how data locality, cache hit ratio, and disk access patterns affect queries on large tables","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Data Modeling & Schema Design for Large Datasets","scope_included":"- Design schemas that support large-scale growth while minimizing hot spots and contention
- Choose appropriate data types with attention to size, alignment, and long-term storage overhead (e.g., `int` vs `bigint`, `uuid`, `jsonb`, `text`, arrays)
- Evaluate normalization vs denormalization trade-offs for large analytic vs OLTP workloads
- Identify when to precompute or materialize aggregates vs compute on the fly for large tables
- Design surrogate keys and primary keys that avoid contention (e.g., monotonic sequences vs random UUIDs) based on workload patterns
- Use foreign keys appropriately in large schemas, understanding their impact on write performance and referential integrity
- Design and manage large lookup / dimension tables, including surrogate keys, indexes, and slowly changing dimensions basics
- Understand when and how to use `jsonb` and semi-structured data at scale, including indexing and performance implications
- Plan for schema evolution on large tables (adding columns, data type changes, default values, constraints) with minimal downtime
- Design audit and history tables that are scalable (append-only patterns, partitioning candidates, retention strategies)
- Recognize patterns that lead to unbounded row growth in hot tables and design archival or roll-up strategies","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Indexing Strategy at Scale","scope_included":"- Select and maintain appropriate indexes for large tables, balancing read performance, write overhead, and storage
- Understand and apply different index types for large datasets: B-tree, GIN, GiST, BRIN, Hash (with focus on B-tree, GIN, BRIN)
- Use multi-column indexes, index-only scans, and covering indexes effectively for large queries
- Understand how index size, correlation, and selectivity affect planner choices and performance
- Design partial indexes for common filtered query patterns to reduce index size and improve performance
- Use expression indexes for computed columns and search conditions that are repeatedly used
- Recognize when BRIN indexes are appropriate for very large, naturally ordered tables (e.g., time-series data)
- Understand GIN index trade-offs for full-text search or `jsonb` queries on large datasets (build time, memory, maintenance)
- Analyze index usage and identify unused or redundant indexes using `pg_stat_user_indexes` and `pg_stat_all_indexes`
- Plan index creation strategies for large tables (concurrent index creation, impact on locks and write performance)
- Manage index maintenance on large tables (REINDEX strategies, avoiding index bloat, monitoring index size growth)
- Evaluate the impact of additional indexes on bulk writes, batch updates, and ETL workloads","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Query Planning & Optimization on Large Tables","scope_included":"- Read and interpret `EXPLAIN` and `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` output for large queries, understanding the main node types (Seq Scan, Index Scan, Index Only Scan, Bitmap Heap Scan, Hash Join, Merge Join, Nested Loop)
- Correlate planner cost estimates with actual timings, rows, and I/O behavior on large tables
- Identify when the planner chooses sequential scans vs index scans and how that changes with table size and statistics
- Use `ANALYZE` and auto-analyze effectively to keep planner statistics meaningful on large, frequently updated tables
- Understand how `default_statistics_target` and extended statistics affect cardinality estimates in complex queries
- Rewrite queries to be more planner-friendly at scale (e.g., avoiding un-sargable predicates, simplifying joins, pushing filters down)
- Recognize and address common anti-patterns on large datasets: unnecessary `SELECT *`, unbounded queries, Cartesian products, non-indexed filters, functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses
- Optimize join orders and join conditions for large joins; understand how join algorithms behave as data size grows
- Use query-level settings (`enable_seqscan`, `enable_nestloop`, etc.) in a diagnostic capacity to understand planner choices
- Apply `LIMIT` and pagination strategies that scale (keyset pagination, avoiding large OFFSETs on huge tables where appropriate)
- Understand how work memory (`work_mem`) and temporary files influence performance of large sorts, hashes, and aggregations
- Identify and mitigate `temp file` usage and disk spillovers for large aggregations and sorts
- Diagnose slow queries using `pg_stat_statements` and other statistics views, focusing on high-impact, frequently executed queries","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Storage, Partitioning & Table Management","scope_included":"- Understand how PostgreSQL stores table data, visibility information, and TOAST data on disk, and how that impacts large tables
- Evaluate when table partitioning is appropriate (data size, retention, query patterns, maintenance needs)
- Implement and manage declarative partitioning (range, list; basic hash understanding) for large tables
- Design partitioning schemes that align with query and retention patterns (e.g., by date, tenant, region)
- Manage lifecycle of partitions: creating new ones, detaching/dropping old partitions, handling archival use cases
- Understand partition pruning and how to write queries and constraints to maximize pruning effectiveness
- Recognize limitations and trade-offs of partitioning (overhead of many partitions, global vs local indexes, query planning overhead)
- Use tablespaces conceptually to separate hot vs cold data, or different storage tiers, and understand their performance implications
- Assess and manage table and index bloat at a high level, understanding common causes on large tables (dead tuples, long transactions)
- Use `pg_class`, `pg_stat_all_tables`, and related views to assess table size, live/ dead tuples, and vacuum status
- Apply CLUSTER or `pg_repack` (or similar tools) conceptually to improve data locality for very large tables when needed
- Plan and execute large-scale schema and storage changes (adding/dropping columns, changing data types) with strategies that minimize downtime and lock contention","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Concurrency, Transactions & Locking at Scale","scope_included":"- Apply PostgreSQL MVCC concepts to reason about read/write behavior on large, highly concurrent tables
- Choose appropriate transaction isolation levels (`read committed`, `repeatable read`, `serializable`) based on workload and contention risks
- Understand how long-running transactions impact vacuum, bloat, and overall performance on large datasets
- Diagnose and resolve row-level and table-level lock contention using `pg_locks` and related views
- Recognize patterns that cause lock escalation, deadlocks, or excessive blocking in large-scale workloads
- Structure batched updates and deletes to minimize locking and avoid long-running transactions on large tables
- Use explicit locking constructs (`FOR UPDATE`, advisory locks) judiciously in large-scale environments
- Understand the impact of DDL operations on locks and concurrency, especially on very large tables
- Design application-level patterns (idempotent operations, retriable transactions) that cooperate well with PostgreSQL locking semantics
- Recognize and mitigate issues caused by hotspots (e.g., single counter rows, contention on nextval, heavily updated rows)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Maintenance, Vacuum & Bloat Management","scope_included":"- Understand how VACUUM and autovacuum work on large tables, including visibility map and freeze concepts at a practical level
- Interpret autovacuum-related configuration parameters and their impact on large tables (scale factors, cost limits, thresholds)
- Identify tables that require adjusted autovacuum settings due to size or update patterns
- Diagnose bloat symptoms using PostgreSQL catalog and monitoring queries (e.g., dead tuple ratios, relation size growth vs live tuples)
- Plan and execute manual VACUUM (including `VACUUM FULL` when appropriate) and REINDEX operations while considering downtime and locks
- Develop operational strategies to avoid wraparound issues in large databases by monitoring transaction ID consumption
- Schedule and coordinate routine maintenance tasks (vacuuming, analyze, reindexing) within maintenance windows for large systems
- Understand the trade-offs of `VACUUM FULL` vs online alternatives (e.g., `pg_repack`) in terms of locks, I/O, and space reclamation
- Integrate maintenance strategies with partitioning and archival/retention policies to keep large datasets manageable","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Data Ingestion, ETL & Bulk Operations","scope_included":"- Use `COPY` and `\\copy` efficiently for bulk data loading, understanding performance and constraint implications
- Plan large INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations to avoid overwhelming autovacuum and generating excessive bloat
- Batch large writes (e.g., chunked operations) to balance throughput and lock durations on large tables
- Decide when to disable or defer non-critical indexes and constraints during large bulk loads and rebuild them afterward
- Understand the impact of triggers, foreign keys, and ON CONFLICT handling on bulk operations at scale
- Use staging tables and ETL patterns to load and transform large datasets with minimal impact on production tables
- Optimize ETL queries for large datasets, including incremental loads, change data capture ingestion, and merge-upsert patterns
- Monitor and control WAL generation during bulk operations, and understand when to adjust related settings within safe boundaries
- Handle large deletes using soft-delete patterns, partition dropping, or archival strategies instead of row-by-row deletion when appropriate
- Understand practical limitations of single transaction size and design ETL processes to avoid transaction bloating and long locks","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"High Availability, Replication & Failover Basics","scope_included":"- Understand physical streaming replication concepts (primary, standby, WAL shipping) at a practical level for large databases
- Recognize how replication lag is affected by write volume, network, and hardware when the primary handles large datasets
- Interpret key replication-related parameters (e.g., synchronous vs asynchronous replication, `max_wal_senders`, `wal_keep_size` at a conceptual level)
- Understand how large databases influence failover time and recovery, especially in terms of WAL replay and checkpointing
- Participate in setting up and maintaining basic streaming replicas for read scaling and high availability (under guidance for complex topologies)
- Recognize the operational impact of large datasets on promotion/failover, including application connection routing and cache warm-up
- Understand logical replication at a conceptual level and its applicability for selective table replication or zero-downtime migrations of large tables
- Help design read-routing patterns that leverage replicas for reporting and analytics on large data, while being aware of replication lag issues
- Contribute to runbooks and processes for controlled failover and switchover in large environments","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Backup, Restore & Disaster Recovery for Large Databases","scope_included":"- Understand trade-offs between logical backups (pg_dump/pg_restore) and physical backups (base backups, file system or backup tools) for large databases
- Participate in configuring and validating physical backup strategies suitable for multi-terabyte datasets (e.g., continuous archiving, incremental backups via external tools)
- Recognize how backup frequency, retention, and WAL archiving affect storage and recovery point objectives (RPO) for large systems
- Estimate and communicate realistic recovery time (RTO) for restoring large databases from backups
- Execute restore and recovery procedures in non-production environments for large datasets (including PITR basics)
- Validate backups through test restores, checksum verification, and basic consistency checks on large restored databases
- Understand how logical backups scale poorly for very large datasets and when they are still useful (e.g., per-database, per-schema migration)
- Assist in designing backup strategies that consider partitioned tables, archival storage, and tiered data retention
- Document and follow runbooks for backup and restore operations, including steps to minimize downtime during recovery","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Monitoring, Observability & Capacity Planning","scope_included":"- Use PostgreSQL statistics views (`pg_stat_database`, `pg_stat_user_tables`, `pg_stat_statements`, etc.) to monitor performance on large instances
- Track and interpret key metrics for large databases: cache hit ratio, active vs idle connections, query latency distributions, dead tuples, autovacuum activity, replication lag, WAL volume
- Identify slow and high-impact queries on large datasets using `pg_stat_statements` and similar tools, and prioritize optimization efforts
- Monitor disk space usage trends for tables, indexes, and WAL, and anticipate capacity issues before they become critical
- Recognize early warning signs of performance degradation specific to large databases (increasing bloat, longer vacuum times, growing temp file usage)
- Work with infrastructure/operations teams to interpret system-level metrics (CPU, memory, I/O, network) in relation to PostgreSQL workload characteristics
- Contribute to setting alerts and thresholds appropriate for large environments (e.g., replication lag limits, disk usage thresholds, autovacuum lag)
- Assist in capacity planning discussions by providing data-based insights on growth rates and performance trends of large tables and indexes","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Security & Compliance Considerations with Large Data","scope_included":"- Apply role-based access control principles for large, multi-application databases, including least privilege for high-volume tables
- Understand how to protect sensitive data at scale using column-level privileges and basic masking patterns (at the application or view level)
- Be aware of encryption considerations for large datasets (disk-level encryption, TLS in transit), and their performance implications in PostgreSQL environments
- Use row-level security (RLS) conceptually for multi-tenant large datasets and understand its potential performance impact
- Recognize the challenges of auditing and logging in large databases (log volume, retention, analysis) and basic strategies to manage them
- Assist in implementing and verifying access policies, particularly for production databases with large and sensitive data
- Understand basic compliance-driven constraints (e.g., retention, right-to-be-forgotten) and how they shape data lifecycle practices for large tables","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling & Ecosystem for Large-Scale PostgreSQL (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Be comfortable using `psql` and core PostgreSQL utilities (`pg_dump`, `pg_restore`, `pg_basebackup`, `vacuumdb`, `reindexdb`, `clusterdb`) in large environments
- Use common GUI or web-based administration tools (e.g., pgAdmin, DBeaver) effectively without relying on them exclusively for critical large-scale operations
- Understand the role of connection poolers (e.g., PgBouncer) in managing high connection counts and how they interact with PostgreSQL at scale
- Be familiar with monitoring stacks commonly used with PostgreSQL (e.g., Prometheus + exporters, Grafana, or equivalent) and navigate dashboards for large systems
- Work with log aggregation and analysis tools to inspect PostgreSQL logs at scale (e.g., ELK/EFK stacks, cloud-native log services)
- Use migration/versioning tools (e.g., Flyway, Liquibase, Sqitch) for controlled schema evolution on large databases
- Be aware of specialized tools for large-scale maintenance and data movement (e.g., `pg_repack`, logical replication managers, dump/restore boosters) and know when to suggest their use
- Integrate PostgreSQL with job/orchestration systems (e.g., cron, Airflow) for large ETL or maintenance workflows, under guidance on complex flows","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Environment Setup & Configuration for Large Instances (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Understand how basic PostgreSQL configuration parameters impact large databases: memory (`shared_buffers`, `work_mem`, `maintenance_work_mem`), WAL (`wal_level`, `max_wal_size`), autovacuum, checkpoints
- Assist in tuning these parameters for large workloads following established guidelines and patterns rather than guesswork
- Recognize when the current hardware (CPU, RAM, storage type, IOPS) is insufficient for the dataset size and workload profile
- Understand the impact of storage choices (HDD vs SSD, RAID levels, cloud volumes) on large PostgreSQL deployments
- Participate in designing database layouts (data directories, WAL directories, tablespaces) to optimize performance and resilience
- Assist in configuring replication and backup components as part of environment setup for large instances
- Support configuration management practices (e.g., using Ansible, Terraform, Helm, cloud-native tooling) to keep large environments consistent
- Verify that logging, monitoring, and backup configurations are correctly applied and working for new or scaled-up environments","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation & Operational Practices (Non-core)","scope_included":"- Document schema designs, partitioning schemes, and indexing strategies specifically with large-scale considerations and rationale
- Contribute to and maintain runbooks for common large-scale operations: index creation, vacuum tuning, bulk loads, failover, restores
- Communicate clearly with application developers about query patterns, anti-patterns, and constraints that matter at scale
- Participate in incident response for performance or availability issues in large PostgreSQL environments, including basic triage and data gathering
- Collaborate with DevOps/SRE and infrastructure teams to coordinate maintenance windows, capacity planning, and environment changes
- Review and give feedback on proposed schema or query changes with an eye toward long-term scalability
- Help onboard less-experienced team members in understanding large-scale PostgreSQL practices and conventions in the organization
- Maintain change logs and documentation for major schema and configuration changes affecting large datasets, to support troubleshooting and audits","is_non_core_section":true}]}
